raw_text:
D. O. Ross, Jr.,'The Culex and Moretum as Post-Augustan Literary
Parodies,' HSCP 79 (1975) 235-63.
Ross identifies Culex and Moretum as clever post-Vergil ian parodies. Culex
poses as a period piece but reflects contemporary (late Tiberian?) poetic
manners; Moretum is a superb parody wherein epic expressions and mannerisms are trivialized by their contexts or by the sense they convey. The
realistic touches are reflections of Alexandrian
and neoteric treatments of
the heroic indomestic settings.

raw_text:
Marisa Squillante, 'Studi sulle Bucoliche e su'VEneide di Virgilio',
BStudLat 5 (1975) 66-77.
Rudd's chapter assesses various principles and arguable designs relating to
the architecture of the Eclogues. Each system contains a defect; none claims a
superiority which might suggest Vergil's handiwork. Rudd assumes that the
final pattern of the book was not a matter of primary importance to Vergil.
Rudd also introduces the first book of Horace's Satires for parallel discussion;
comparable questions arise. Scholars have been more concerned with the
texture than with the architecture of the collection. Rudd's criteria for the
poet's blueprint are cogent: the features of the plan should be large, bold, and
simple, certainly discernible when pointed out. 'The more subtle an architectural
theory is, and
the more it tries to explain, the less persuasive it
becomes.' Kollmann considers the function and place of Proper Names inthe
Bucolics where their appearance is exceptional. Kollmann assigns the names
to four groups according to the nature of their content: (1) Poetry, Song; (2)
Love; (3) Shepherds and their life; and (4) Idyllic world vs. Reality. Names
communicate associations and rouse the emotions; they activate information
and stimulate reactions. The Theocritean contribution of Proper Names is
relatively small. The articles by Boyle and Coleman are required reading for
students of the telogues. Schmidt's exercise with the chronological problems
attaching to the Bucolics is intricate and somewhat defeating; Rudd's chapter
is a useful companion piece.
18.
Hans-Joachim
Glücklich,
'Der Übersetzungskommentar,' Der Altsprachliche Unterricht 18 (1975) 5-18.

yÇ^ùcklich's article examines Eclogues I, 1-15. Putnam's article offers fresh
and perceptive insights into the nature of the first Bucolic. Van Sickle's study
partly complements and enlarges Putnam's argument. Gordon and Deryck
Williams offer highly
instructive analyses of the Messianic Eclogue. R. D.

in Ancient and Modern Studies (ed. Louis L. Orlin), (Ann Arbor, Mich.,
1975)
171-99,
with
Response
by Charles Wittke, 201-3, and Discussion,
203-10.
Putnam's scrutiny of Georgics 2, 136-76 (Laudes Ita/iae) and the end of
Georgias 2 (with pertinent epic passages) detects ambiguities inV's feelings
about Rome as a political entity. Human nature's imperfections trammel and
embarrass Saturnia teiius and the peoples of Italy. The same tension between
order and disorder, evident in the symbolism of violence, suppressed or rampant, pervades the Aeneid. Putnam finds Vergil repeatedly preoccupied with
thè reality of power's misuse,
the innocent Eden invaded and even militarized. Putnam's article challenges the 'idealistic' eulogy with disturbing ambiguities which, in his view, surface repeatedly in the 'natural' and 'human'
setting.

Putnam's scrutiny of Georgics 2, 136-76 (Laudes Ita/iae) and the end of
Georgias 2 (with pertinent epic passages) detects ambiguities inV's feelings
about Rome as a political entity. Human nature's imperfections trammel and
embarrass Saturnia teiius and the peoples of Italy. The same tension between
order and disorder, evident in the symbolism of violence, suppressed or ram-
pant, pervades the Aeneid. Putnam finds Vergil repeatedly preoccupied with
thè reality of power's misuse,
the innocent Eden invaded and even mili-
tarized. Putnam's article challenges the 'idealistic' eulogy with disturbing am-
biguities which, in his view, surface repeatedly in the 'natural' and 'human'
setting.

raw_text:
Grace Starry West, Women in
Vergil's Aeneid. Diss., University of
California, Los Angeles, 1975. Summary inDA 36 (1975) 2800A-2801 A.
Hanson's essay examines aspects of Aeneas which seem to impede his
mission and contradict Jupiter's optimism. A comparison of Aeneas with
Odysseus discloses marked differences between the heroes: in self-control,
intellectual
awareness,
and
sympathy
with
women.
Aeneas,
for Hanson,
shows no maturation or change: he remains 'a man who lacks self-control,
who cannot comprehend reality, and who cannot deal with the feminine half
of human life.'
